cards.headline: Iran begins burying Khamenei with his successor still in hiding, a Category-5 typhoon slams America's Pacific islands, and Mumbai shuts down under torrential rain — as diplomats converge on Ankara to talk about ending the Ukraine war.
cards.big_picture: Today is a day of tests of who is actually in charge. Iran is staging the largest day of a week-long state funeral for its assassinated Supreme Leader while the son chosen to succeed him has not been seen in public since February — a superpower standoff with an invisible man at its centre. France finds out tomorrow whether its most popular far-right politician is allowed to run for president at all, and Donald Trump spent the weekend on the phone with both Putin and Zelensky ahead of a NATO summit that starts tomorrow in Ankara. Nature, meanwhile, is running its own stress tests: a Category-5 typhoon is crossing America's Pacific islands this morning, Mumbai has closed its schools after rain killed at least seven people, and Europe is fighting a culture war over air conditioning in record heat. For India it is an unusually consequential Monday — Modi begins a three-nation Indo-Pacific tour, the US case against Gautam Adani moves toward dismissal, and markets reopen to a calmer oil map than the one they feared a fortnight ago.
cards.market_headline: Reopen day: after a long holiday weekend, US and Indian markets trade the weekend's relief — a calmer Hormuz, peace calls on Ukraine, the Adani case near dismissal — with Fed minutes Wednesday and TCS kicking off India's earnings season Thursday.

cards.title: Iran buries Khamenei — while the successor, his son, stays invisible
cards.what_happened: Iran's week-long state funeral (3–9 July) reached its largest days, with millions expected in Tehran and processions planned through Qom and the Iraqi holy cities of Najaf and Karbala before burial in Mashhad. On Sunday three of Khamenei's sons prayed beside his coffin — but Mojtaba, the one who now holds his father's job, did not appear. The ceremony unfolded amid fresh rhetoric: Donald Trump warned Iran the US needs only 'one shot', and Tehran replied that America 'can kill people, but not ideals.'
cards.moot: In personalised political systems, the person IS the institution — when a leader dies or disappears, the uncertainty isn't just about one job but about whether the whole system can still make decisions. Markets and rivals price that uncertainty immediately.

depth.background: Ayatollah Ali Khamenei ruled Iran for more than three decades as Supreme Leader — the country's highest religious and political authority, above the president. He was killed in a US-Israeli airstrike on 28 February, at the very start of this year's war between Iran and the US-Israel alliance. His 56-year-old son Mojtaba was hastily elected Supreme Leader in March, but he was reportedly badly wounded in the same attack (which also killed his mother and his wife) and has not appeared in public since — he communicates only through written statements.
depth.why_it_matters: A country of 90 million people, sitting on one of the world's biggest oil and gas reserves, is being led by a man nobody has seen for four months. That is an extraordinary vacuum at the top of a state that just fought a war with the United States and Israel. The funeral is also a political performance: the Islamic Republic is using religious imagery to project continuity and defiance at the exact moment its leadership looks most fragile.
depth.ripple_effects: If Mojtaba cannot rule visibly, factions inside Iran — the Revolutionary Guards, rival clerics — will compete to fill the space, and that competition could pull Iran toward either accommodation with the West or renewed confrontation. Every neighbour, oil trader and foreign ministry is watching the same question: who actually gives orders in Tehran? The answer determines whether the fragile ceasefire and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz (the sea lane carrying about a fifth of the world's oil) hold.
depth.why_now: The funeral was delayed more than four months by the war and mourning rules; this week's ceremonies are the regime's first big set-piece since the ceasefire — and Mojtaba's absence from his own father's funeral turned a memorial into a succession mystery.
depth.watch_next: Whether Mojtaba Khamenei appears (or even speaks) before the burial in Mashhad on 9 July — and how Washington responds if he does not.
depth.market_link: Any sign of chaos or hardline consolidation in Tehran would put a risk premium back into oil (WTI sits oversold near $68.78) — a direct concern for oil-importer India.

key_terms.definition: Iran's highest authority — a cleric who outranks the elected president and controls the military, judiciary and state media.
An agreed halt to fighting that stops short of a formal peace deal — it can collapse if either side stops honouring it.

cards.title: Super Typhoon Bavi, a Category-5 monster, crosses Guam and the Marianas today
cards.what_happened: Super Typhoon Bavi is making its closest pass over the islands this Monday morning local time, with forecast landfall-strength winds around 165 mph (265+ km/h), gusts well above that, waves up to 11 metres (35 feet), and 300–500 mm of rain — most of it inside a single 12-hour window. Evacuations were ordered across Guam, and the most likely impact zone runs between Guam and the islands of Tinian and Saipan.
cards.moot: Warm oceans are storm fuel — as seas heat up, the strongest storm categories become more common, and places hit repeatedly suffer more than the sum of each hit because recovery never finishes. Disasters compound.

depth.background: Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands are small US territories in the western Pacific, home to about 220,000 people and major American military bases. The region is typhoon country, but storms of the very top category — 5, meaning sustained winds above 157 mph — are rare direct hits. Bavi is already the third Category-5 storm of 2026, a pace scientists link to unusually warm ocean water, which is the fuel typhoons feed on.
depth.why_it_matters: Winds at Category-5 strength don't just damage buildings — they can destroy water, power and communications for weeks on islands that can't easily be resupplied. These islands were already battered by typhoons earlier this season, so this is a compounding disaster: infrastructure weakened once is hit again before it has healed.
depth.ripple_effects: The US military's key western-Pacific hub may be degraded for a period. After the Marianas, forecasts steer Bavi toward Taiwan by around 11 July — putting the world's most important semiconductor-manufacturing island on storm watch next. Insurance costs for Pacific territories keep ratcheting up, a quiet tax that climate change levies on island life.
depth.why_now: Peak Pacific typhoon season plus record-warm sea surface temperatures — the same warm-ocean signal behind this year's unusual number of top-category storms.
depth.watch_next: Damage reports from Guam and Saipan over the next 24 hours, and whether Bavi holds its strength on the forecast track toward Taiwan (~11 July).
depth.market_link: Minimal direct market impact today, but if Bavi tracks into Taiwan next weekend it becomes a watch item for chipmakers and their supply chains.

key_terms.definition: The top of the hurricane/typhoon scale — sustained winds above 157 mph, capable of catastrophic destruction.
Seawater pushed ashore by a storm's winds and low pressure — often the deadliest part of a typhoon, not the wind itself.

cards.title: Mumbai shuts schools as monsoon fury kills at least seven — a chawl collapses in Mankhurd
cards.what_happened: Torrential rain battered Mumbai and the wider Maharashtra coast on Sunday. A multi-storey chawl collapsed in Mankhurd (which recorded ~134 mm of rain), killing at least six people; a 63-year-old man died when a tree fell on a shop in Kurla. Several areas crossed 200 mm of rain under an IMD red alert, flight operations at Mumbai airport were halted for about an hour, and the city has declared a holiday for all schools and colleges today (Monday, 6 July) as the IMD keeps an orange alert in place.
cards.moot: Extreme rain doesn't strike a city evenly — it finds the weakest structures and the poorest neighbourhoods first. Climate resilience is really a housing and drainage story, and the monsoon is as much an economic event as a weather event.

depth.background: Mumbai's monsoon (June–September) is both a lifeline and an annual hazard: the city depends on the rains, but its drainage, built for a smaller city, is overwhelmed when 200+ mm falls in a day. 'Chawls' are Mumbai's old, low-cost multi-storey housing blocks — single-room tenements with shared balconies — many of them decades old and structurally fragile, housing some of the city's poorest residents.
depth.why_it_matters: For India's financial capital, this is the season's first full-scale stress test — and the deaths show the human cost concentrates in the oldest, poorest housing. Beyond Mumbai, how the monsoon behaves matters to the whole economy: it decides crop output, rural incomes and food prices for hundreds of millions.
depth.ripple_effects: Expect disrupted commutes, flight delays and possible fresh building-safety orders in Mumbai this week. Economically, a strong monsoon is usually good news for India (it waters the kharif — summer-sown — crop), but destructive cloudbursts in cities cut activity and can damage goods and infrastructure. If heavy rain persists, watch vegetable prices — the fastest way weather becomes inflation.
depth.why_now: Peak monsoon onset over the Konkan coast; the IMD had a red alert on Mumbai through the weekend and has kept an orange alert for today.
depth.watch_next: Whether the IMD extends alerts beyond Monday, and the structural-safety response to the Mankhurd chawl collapse.
depth.market_link: A monsoon that turns destructive (or deficient) feeds food inflation, which shapes RBI rate policy — one of the swing factors for Indian markets this quarter.

key_terms.definition: An old Mumbai housing block of single-room tenements with shared corridors — cheap, crowded and often structurally weak.
India's weather-warning scale: orange means 'be prepared' for very heavy rain; red means 'take action' — extremely heavy rain likely.

cards.title: Trump works the phones with Putin and Zelensky ahead of tomorrow's NATO summit
cards.what_happened: Trump held a roughly 90-minute call with Vladimir Putin on 4 July — described by Kremlin aide Yury Ushakov as 'businesslike and quite constructive' — in which Trump offered to help find a settlement; the Kremlin said it seeks a 'political-diplomatic resolution.' Trump also spoke with Volodymyr Zelensky, who called it 'a very good call' and said 'there is a real prospect to put an end to this war.' All of this lands ahead of the NATO summit in Ankara on 7–8 July.
cards.moot: Wars move toward negotiation when the battlefield leverage shifts, not when the talking starts — diplomacy usually ratifies facts created on the ground rather than replacing them.

depth.background: Russia's full-scale war on Ukraine is in its fifth year, but the battlefield balance has been shifting: Ukraine has demonstrated it can strike military and energy targets deep inside Russia (it burned a major Russian oil terminal last week), eroding what Moscow assumed was a safe rear. Donald Trump has repeatedly offered himself as the dealmaker who ends the war; his envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner have been shuttling toward Moscow for months.
depth.why_it_matters: This is the most explicit peace choreography in months, and it is happening because the leverage has moved: Russia's energy infrastructure is now vulnerable, its ally Iran is weakened, and both sides have reasons to test a deal. Whether talk becomes negotiation depends on the terms — territory, security guarantees for Ukraine, and sanctions relief for Russia are the hard parts no phone call resolves.
depth.ripple_effects: A genuine peace track would reshape energy markets (Russian oil and gas flows), European defence budgets, and grain trade. A failed one could mean escalation instead — Ukraine has new deep-strike reach, and Russia will be tempted to answer. For India, which buys discounted Russian crude, any settlement that normalises Russian oil would quietly rearrange its energy bill.
depth.why_now: The call was timed just before the Ankara NATO summit — Trump wants to arrive with momentum, and both Moscow and Kyiv want to shape the agenda rather than react to it.
depth.watch_next: The NATO summit in Ankara on 7–8 July: whether it produces a concrete negotiating framework or just communiqués.
depth.market_link: Peace prospects press on oil and European gas prices, lift European equities, and would weigh on defence stocks; failure does the reverse.

key_terms.definition: The Western military alliance of 32 countries built on collective defence — an attack on one member is treated as an attack on all.
A binding promise by outside powers to defend a country if attacked — the core of what Ukraine wants from any peace deal.

cards.title: The Adani case heads for dismissal — but a US judge wants answers by 13 July
cards.what_happened: The DoJ has formally urged the court to dismiss the charges permanently, saying the case 'should never have been brought': the conduct happened almost entirely in India, Indian authorities found no actionable misconduct, investors lost no money, and the evidence sits abroad — adding that acting as the 'world police' can cause diplomatic strife. But District Judge Nicholas Garaufis called the DoJ's explanation 'terse, bland and conclusory' and ordered a fuller justification by 13 July. US media reports note Adani hired Robert Giuffra Jr — one of Trump's personal lawyers — and that his team floated a $10-billion US investment pledge while lobbying the DoJ; the DoJ says the decision was not tied to investments.
cards.moot: For global companies, legal risk lives at the intersection of law and politics — a change of government can change a case's fate, which is why markets price 'jurisdiction risk' and why judges demand reasons when prosecutors reverse course.

depth.background: In 2024, US prosecutors under the Biden administration indicted Gautam Adani — one of India's two richest industrialists — and seven associates, alleging a scheme to bribe Indian officials with about $250 million to win solar-power contracts while raising money from US investors. Adani has always denied wrongdoing. In May 2026 the Trump-era Department of Justice moved to drop the case entirely; the change of administration changed the case's fate.
depth.why_it_matters: For India's markets and business establishment, the near-certain end of this case removes a cloud that has hung over the Adani group — ports, airports, power, cement — for two years. For observers of US justice, it is a test case of whether prosecutorial decisions now track political relationships: the reported investment offer and the Trump-linked legal team are exactly why the judge is demanding a paper trail.
depth.ripple_effects: Adani group companies could re-rate (investors paying more per rupee of earnings) as legal risk fades, and the group's access to global capital reopens. Diplomatically, it clears an irritant in US-India relations at a moment both sides are negotiating on trade. The judge's demand keeps a small chance alive that the dismissal comes with conditions or embarrassing disclosures.
depth.why_now: The DoJ's detailed justification is due to Judge Garaufis by 13 July — this week decides whether the dismissal is rubber-stamped or scrutinised.
depth.watch_next: The DoJ's 13 July filing and whether the judge grants dismissal 'with prejudice' (permanently, so charges can't be refiled).
depth.market_link: Adani group stocks are the direct play; more broadly it supports foreign-investor sentiment toward Indian large-caps.

key_terms.definition: A formal criminal charge — an accusation to be tested at trial, not a finding of guilt.
Ending a case permanently, so the same charges cannot be brought again.

cards.title: France learns tomorrow whether Le Pen can run for president
cards.what_happened: The Paris Court of Appeal delivers its ruling on Tuesday, 7 July. Prosecutors have recommended a four-year sentence (one year to be served, possibly under electronic monitoring), a €100,000 fine — and a five-year ban on holding public office, which is the part that would end her 2027 candidacy.
cards.moot: In modern democracies, courtrooms can matter as much as ballot boxes — a single legal ruling can remove a frontrunner and reroute a country's politics, which is why 'political risk' includes judicial calendars.

depth.background: Marine Le Pen has led France's far right for over a decade and came closer to the presidency with each of her three runs. In 2025 a court convicted her of embezzling European Parliament funds — using EU money meant for parliamentary assistants to pay party staff — and, crucially, barred her from public office. She appealed; without a reversal she cannot contest the 2027 election, widely seen as her best-ever chance to succeed Emmanuel Macron.
depth.why_it_matters: This is one court decision that could redraw European politics: polls have long placed Le Pen among the favourites for 2027. If she is barred, her 30-year-old protégé Jordan Bardella likely becomes the far right's candidate — younger, less tested, but currently popular. The case also sharpens a debate democracies keep having: courts enforcing the law against politicians versus voters' right to choose whom they want.
depth.ripple_effects: A ban would trigger accusations of political justice and could energise her base behind Bardella; an acquittal instantly makes her the 2027 frontrunner and unsettles mainstream parties. Investors watch French elections closely because far-right fiscal plans have previously rattled the bond market — the market where governments borrow.
depth.why_now: The appeals court set 7 July as its ruling date back in February; the decision lands tomorrow.
depth.watch_next: Tuesday's ruling — specifically whether the public-office ban is upheld, softened, or overturned.
depth.market_link: French government bonds and the euro are sensitive to any outcome that raises the odds of a far-right, high-spending presidency in 2027.

key_terms.definition: A higher court that re-examines a conviction or sentence — it can uphold, change or cancel the original decision.
A legal bar on holding or running for public office, sometimes imposed as part of a criminal sentence.

cards.title: America's jobs engine is sputtering — and this week the new Fed chair shows his cards
cards.what_happened: June's jobs report showed the US added just 57,000 jobs — about half the ~115,000 expected. The unemployment rate FELL to 4.2%, but for a bad reason: so many people stopped looking for work that labour-force participation hit its lowest level in 50 years outside the Covid era (you only count as unemployed if you're actively searching). This week Wall Street returns from the long July-4th weekend to a light calendar whose main event is Wednesday's minutes from the June Fed meeting — the first under Warsh.
cards.moot: Never read one number alone: the unemployment rate can 'improve' because people gave up, not because they found jobs. Participation tells you whether a falling jobless rate is good news or a warning.

depth.background: The US central bank (the Federal Reserve) sets interest rates to balance inflation against jobs; the whole world watches because dollar rates set the price of money everywhere. The Fed has been holding rates high to fight inflation that is still running near 4.2%. Kevin Warsh recently took over as Fed chair — and he has already signalled he dislikes central-bank forecasting rituals like the 'dot plot' of rate projections.
depth.why_it_matters: Weak hiring kills the fear of further rate HIKES — that relief is what pushed the Dow to a record last week. But sticky ~4.2% inflation means no cuts either: the Fed is stuck 'higher for longer.' The deeper worry is what falling participation says — an economy where people give up looking for work is cooling in a way a single unemployment number hides.
depth.ripple_effects: For the US: cheaper borrowing hopes stay parked, and a slowing consumer eventually hits company profits. For India: a Fed on hold keeps the dollar firm and the rupee under pressure near record lows (~95 per dollar), and a cooling US consumer is a slow headwind for Indian IT and export earnings. Gold — which pays no interest and so shines when rate rises fade — has stayed firm near $4,187.
depth.why_now: Markets reopen today after the holiday; Wednesday's Fed minutes are the first written window into how the Warsh-era Fed actually reasons.
depth.watch_next: Wednesday's June Fed minutes, then the 29 July Fed decision — and whether the next US inflation print revives hike fears or opens the door to cuts.
depth.market_link: Rates-sensitive everything: US stocks and gold ride the 'no more hikes' relief; the firm dollar keeps the rupee near record lows and pressures foreign flows into India.

key_terms.definition: The share of adults either working or actively job-hunting — it can fall (and flatter the unemployment rate) when discouraged people simply stop looking.
The detailed written record of a Federal Reserve meeting, released three weeks later — traders mine it for clues about future rate moves.

cards.title: Modi begins a three-nation Indo-Pacific tour today — including the first PM visit to New Zealand in ~40 years
cards.what_happened: Prime Minister Modi departs today on a three-nation tour: Indonesia (6–8 July, with talks in Jakarta and a visit to the Prambanan temple complex in Yogyakarta), Australia (8–10 July, including the third India-Australia Annual Summit in Melbourne), and New Zealand (10–11 July) — the first visit by an Indian prime minister in nearly four decades. The agenda centres on maritime security, defence, trade, investment and technology.
cards.moot: Middle powers don't pick one side — they build overlapping webs of trade and security partnerships, so no single relationship can hold them hostage. Diplomacy is diversification.

depth.background: India's 'Act East' policy is its long-running effort to deepen trade and security ties across Southeast Asia and the Pacific, largely as a counterweight to China's growing naval and economic reach. India signed a free-trade agreement with New Zealand in April 2026 — one of its fastest-negotiated deals — and holds annual summits with Australia, a fellow member of the Quad security grouping.
depth.why_it_matters: This is India positioning itself as the Indo-Pacific's swing power. Indonesia is Southeast Asia's biggest economy and guards key sea lanes; Australia is a defence partner and critical-minerals supplier; New Zealand is a fresh FTA partner. Together the trip stitches India's economic and security webs across the region — with China's shadow the unspoken reason everyone is eager to host.
depth.ripple_effects: Expect deliverables in defence cooperation, shipping and ports, critical minerals (Australia supplies inputs for batteries and chips), and diaspora-friendly measures. For businesses, summit outcomes often translate into market access and investment corridors — the India-NZ FTA due for implementation later this year is the concrete example.
depth.why_now: The tour starts today; the Melbourne summit is a scheduled annual fixture, and India wants momentum on regional trade while its deals with the UK (live 15 July) and New Zealand come on stream.
depth.watch_next: Concrete agreements out of the Melbourne India-Australia summit (8–10 July) — especially anything on defence, minerals or trade.
depth.market_link: Watch export-linked Indian sectors (defence, engineering, dairy/agri around the NZ FTA) for summit-driven news; the trade-pact drumbeat supports sentiment more than any single stock.

key_terms.definition: The strategic region spanning the Indian and Pacific Oceans — the arena where the US, China, India and allies compete for influence and secure shipping lanes.
A pact between countries that cuts tariffs (import taxes) and eases rules so more goods and services can flow between them.

cards.title: Record heat scorches two continents — and Europe starts a culture war over air conditioning
cards.what_happened: Germany hit unprecedented highs — 41.7C in Brandenburg on Sunday — as another heatwave builds; the UK issued a week-long health alert with temperatures heading for 34C. The scramble to cool down has turned political: a Guardian report describes an 'AC culture war' over whether air conditioning is a lifesaver or an energy-hogging surrender, while US heat advisories put the heat index at ~106°F in the Mid-Atlantic. One trade-war twist: a historic heatwave has driven record European demand for Chinese-made air conditioners — just as Brussels tries to rebalance trade with Beijing.
cards.moot: Climate change shows up as an infrastructure bill: societies built for yesterday's weather must now retrofit grids, homes and habits — and who pays for adaptation becomes a political fight everywhere.

depth.background: Europe has historically lived without air conditioning — mild summers made it unnecessary, and energy-conscious culture made it unfashionable. That assumption is dying: June temperature records were smashed across the continent (the UK broke its June record at 37.7C), and France logged 2,025 excess deaths at the peak of its last heatwave. In the US, a dangerous heat dome sat over the July-4th holiday.
depth.why_it_matters: Heat is now Europe's deadliest weather — those 2,025 French excess deaths are a bigger toll than most storms — and the infrastructure (grids, homes, hospitals, railways) was built for a climate that no longer exists. The AC debate is really a debate about adaptation: cooling saves lives but strains power grids and raises emissions unless the electricity is clean.
depth.ripple_effects: Electricity demand spikes are stressing grids on both continents (the US Department of Energy even deleted thousands of energy-conservation web pages amid a political row over asking people to raise their thermostats). Sustained heat lifts power prices, cuts outdoor-work productivity, stresses crops, and — via the AC import surge — hands China more leverage in EU trade talks.
depth.why_now: Peak Northern-Hemisphere summer, with back-to-back heatwaves compressing what used to be once-a-decade extremes into successive weeks.
depth.watch_next: Whether this week's European heatwave breaks national July records — and how grid operators cope with record cooling demand.
depth.market_link: Heatwaves lift power prices and utilities' load, boost cooling-appliance makers, and add a weather premium to European energy markets.

key_terms.definition: Deaths above the normal expected number for a period — the standard way to count a heatwave's true toll, since heat rarely appears on death certificates.
What the temperature 'feels like' once humidity is added — high humidity stops sweat from cooling you, making the same temperature more dangerous.

cards.title: India's E20 petrol backlash grows: most drivers surveyed say mileage has dropped
cards.what_happened: A LocalCircles survey found 53% of vehicle owners rate the E20 rollout negatively — many calling it 'disastrous' — and 66% report a drop in mileage (kilometres per litre). Automakers and analysts counter that E20 fuel is compliant and thoroughly tested, and manufacturers have described service protocols for affected vehicles. The row has been building for weeks as more drivers connect fuel-economy complaints to the blend.
cards.moot: Every energy transition redistributes costs — a policy that saves a country money in aggregate can still cost individual households at the pump, and unmeasured trade-offs are where public backlash starts.

depth.background: India blends ethanol (an alcohol fuel distilled mostly from sugarcane and grain) into petrol to cut its huge oil-import bill, support farmers, and lower emissions. The blend has been ratcheted up over years to 'E20' — 20% ethanol — now the standard pump fuel. The catch: ethanol carries less energy per litre than petrol, and older engines weren't designed for it.
depth.why_it_matters: This is a policy with real winners and losers: the country saves foreign exchange on crude imports and sugarcane farmers gain a market, but drivers of older vehicles may quietly pay via more frequent fill-ups — a hidden per-kilometre cost increase. Public trust in the transition depends on honest measurement of that trade-off, and the survey suggests trust is fraying.
depth.ripple_effects: Political pressure to slow the blending ramp or compensate consumers; scrutiny of fuel-economy claims; and commercial stakes for oil-marketing companies, sugar/ethanol producers and automakers (who must certify engines for E20). If mileage losses are real at scale, effective fuel demand rises slightly — partly offsetting the import savings the policy chases.
depth.why_now: The LocalCircles survey published Sunday gave the simmering complaints a number — and the industry pushed back the same weekend.
depth.watch_next: Whether the government orders an independent mileage study or adjusts the blending roadmap in response.
depth.market_link: Touches oil-marketing companies, sugar/ethanol stocks and automakers — and, at the margin, India's crude-import bill.

key_terms.definition: Petrol blended with 20% ethanol — cheaper and cleaner-burning per litre, but with less energy, which can mean fewer kilometres per litre.
How far a vehicle travels per litre of fuel — a small percentage drop compounds into real money over a year of driving.

cards.title: EasyJet agrees in principle to a £5.5bn takeover — Britain's biggest budget airline goes private
cards.what_happened: On Sunday evening easyJet announced an 'agreement in principle' to accept Castlelake's £5.5bn offer, which would take the airline off the London stock market and into private ownership. Final terms still need to be formalised and approved by shareholders and regulators.
cards.moot: When a stock market persistently undervalues companies, private buyers step in to arbitrage the gap — a wave of take-privates is often the market's way of saying prices were too low.

depth.background: EasyJet is the UK's largest low-cost carrier, a listed company since 2000 and a staple of European travel. Castlelake, a US investment firm that specialises in aviation assets, has pursued it for months — easyJet's board rejected four earlier offers before this one.
depth.why_it_matters: A household-name airline leaving the public market is part of a bigger pattern: private investment firms buying UK-listed companies they consider undervalued, shrinking the London stock exchange. For a 'smart but new' reader, this is what 'take-private' means — public shareholders are bought out at a premium, and the company's future plays out away from daily market scrutiny.
depth.ripple_effects: Other UK mid-cap companies get re-examined as takeover candidates, which can lift their share prices. For passengers little changes immediately, but private owners often push harder on costs and fleet strategy. For London's market, each departure renews the debate about why UK shares trade cheaply enough to attract these bids.
depth.why_now: After four rejected offers, Castlelake finally met the board's price — the announcement came Sunday evening to precede Monday's market open.
depth.watch_next: The formal offer document and shareholder vote — and whether any rival bidder emerges.
depth.market_link: Airline and UK mid-cap stocks may firm as investors hunt the next takeover candidate.

key_terms.definition: When an investor buys all of a listed company's shares and removes it from the stock exchange — public shareholders are paid out, usually at a premium.
A deal's outline that both sides intend to complete — key terms agreed, but not yet legally final.

cards.title: Scientists may have found how Alzheimer's spreads through the brain — and a way to block it
cards.what_happened: New research suggests a common brain protein acts as an unexpected courier, packaging toxic Tau proteins and carrying them from damaged neurons into healthy ones — effectively the disease's delivery service. In lab work, blocking these harmful protein packages before they reached new cells stopped the spread, pointing to a possible way to slow the disease's progression.
cards.moot: In medicine, knowing HOW a disease moves is as valuable as knowing what causes it — mechanisms are what drugs can actually target, which is why basic research keeps paying off decades later.

depth.background: Alzheimer's disease, the most common cause of dementia, affects tens of millions of people worldwide, and every family touched by it knows the pattern: it starts with memory and spreads relentlessly. Scientists have long known that a protein called Tau turns toxic and accumulates in dying brain cells — but not how the damage travels from neuron to neuron.
depth.why_it_matters: Most Alzheimer's drugs so far try to clear the junk after it accumulates, with modest results. Identifying the transport mechanism gives medicine a different kind of target: interrupt the courier, and you might freeze the disease where it is rather than chase it after it spreads. It's early — cell and animal work, not a human therapy — but it's a genuinely new angle.
depth.ripple_effects: If the mechanism holds up in humans, it opens a new drug-development race and could eventually change how early-stage patients are treated — slowing progression matters enormously even without a cure, because Alzheimer's costs (care hours, lost independence) scale with severity.
depth.why_now: The findings were published in the past week and are gathering attention as one of the clearest mechanistic leads in years.
depth.watch_next: Whether the result replicates and moves toward human trials — the usual multi-year gap between mechanism and medicine.

key_terms.definition: A protein that normally stabilises brain-cell structure but, in Alzheimer's, misfolds into toxic tangles that kill neurons.
A brain cell that carries signals — the basic wiring of thought and memory.

cards.title: Markets
cards.subtitle: Reopen day: after a long holiday weekend, US and Indian markets trade the weekend's relief — a calmer Hormuz, peace calls on Ukraine, the Adani case near dismissal — with Fed minutes Wednesday and TCS kicking off India's earnings season Thursday.

cards.story: Both big markets reopen today after the long weekend — US exchanges were shut Friday for July 4th and India's over Saturday–Sunday — so the first job of the day is simply to price three days of news. Most of that news was friendly: the Strait of Hormuz (the sea gate for a fifth of the world's oil) keeps reopening and crude sits quiet and oversold near $68.78; Trump held 'constructive' peace calls with both Putin and Zelensky before tomorrow's NATO summit; and the US case against Gautam Adani moved closer to permanent dismissal. The mood-setter underneath is still the weak US June jobs report (just 57,000 jobs added, half of what was expected) — 'bad news' that markets read as good because it buries the fear of another Fed rate hike, even though sticky ~4.2% inflation means no cuts either. That mix keeps money rotating: old-economy blue-chips (the Dow) sit at a record while expensive AI-chip names lag, and some global money is treating India as the calmer harbour — India's own fear gauge is near multi-month lows (~11.8) and Gift Nifty points to a flattish, slightly soft open. The real test for India starts Thursday, when TCS opens the April–June (Q1 FY27) results season: earnings, not headlines, will decide whether the Nifty's firm close (~24,271) is backed by profits. Wednesday's Fed minutes — the first under new chair Kevin Warsh — are the week's other must-read.
themes: Reopen / weekend-news catch-up: Three days of mostly friendly news gets priced at today's open: Hormuz reopening holds, Trump-Putin-Zelensky peace calls before tomorrow's NATO summit, the Adani case near dismissal, and easyJet's £5.5bn take-private. The risk: calm gauges (VIX ~16, India VIX ~11.8) leave little cushion if any of it reverses.

India into earnings: TCS opens the Q1 FY27 season Thursday (~Jul 9); the India–UK trade pact goes live Jul 15 and the Adani judge's deadline is Jul 13. Firm indices on domestic buying now need profits to back them; offsets are the ~95.2 rupee and Mumbai's monsoon disruption (schools shut, city under orange alert).

Rates / the Fed: Weak June jobs (57k) killed the July hike fear, but sticky ~4.2% inflation blocks cuts — 'higher for longer.' Wednesday's June-meeting minutes, the first under new chair Kevin Warsh, are the week's read on how the new Fed actually thinks.

AI chips / US tech: The rotation out of expensive AI names continues to hang over the Nasdaq: Nvidia reopens at $194.83, pinned between its 50-day (~$210) and 200-day (~$191) averages — that $191 line is the one that matters.
Crypto: Bitcoin gave back its weekend firmness (-0.67% to ~$62.7k). The stabilization above short-term averages is being tested; a slip below ~$62.4k support would say the bounce failed.
cards.moot: Reopen day: after a long holiday weekend, US and Indian markets trade the weekend's relief — a calmer Hormuz, peace calls on Ukraine, the Adani case near dismissal — with Fed minutes Wednesday and TCS kicking off India's earnings season Thursday.

global_summary.us: US markets reopen today after the July 4th holiday at last Thursday's closes: S&P 500 ~7,483 (flat), Dow at a record ~52,900 (+1.14%, RSI ~70 overbought), Nasdaq ~25,833 (-0.8%), VIX low at ~16. The tape is split — value leading, AI-chips lagging — on a weak jobs report (57k) that killed rate-hike fears. This week: June Fed minutes Wednesday (first under chair Warsh), jobless claims, and early Q2 reports (Delta, PepsiCo).
global_summary.india: Indian markets reopen after the weekend from a firm Friday close — Nifty ~24,271 (+0.39%), Sensex ~77,764 (+0.34%), Bank Nifty ~57,939 (-0.16%) — with Gift Nifty hinting flat-to-soft and India VIX very low (~11.8). The week turns on Q1 earnings (TCS ~Jul 9), the Adani filing (Jul 13) and the UK trade pact (Jul 15); Mumbai's monsoon disruption and the ~95.2 rupee are the local frictions.
global_summary.crypto: Bitcoin faded to ~$62,669 (-0.67%), giving back the weekend's gains and testing its ~$62.4k support inside a downtrend (below 50- and 200-day averages); Ether flat at ~$1,778. Stabilization is on probation, not confirmed.
global_summary.macro: Crude quiet and oversold at $68.78 as the Hormuz reopening holds and Ukraine peace calls add downside pressure; gold firm near $4,187 on the no-hikes backdrop; rupee ~95.2. Key macro dates: NATO summit Jul 7–8, Fed minutes Jul 8 (US time), Fed decision Jul 29.

catalysts_today: All day — US and Indian markets REOPEN after the long holiday weekend — first pricing of Hormuz, Ukraine-peace and Adani news
Today — PM Modi begins Indo-Pacific tour in Indonesia (then Australia summit Jul 8-10, New Zealand Jul 10-11)
Today (Pacific) — Super Typhoon Bavi (Cat 5) crosses Guam/Marianas; forecast track approaches Taiwan ~Jul 11 — chip-supply watch item
Tuesday Jul 7 — Paris appeals-court verdict on Marine Le Pen's eligibility — French bonds/euro sensitivity; NATO summit opens in Ankara (Jul 7-8)
Wednesday Jul 8 (US time) — June Fed meeting minutes — first under new chair Kevin Warsh
Thursday Jul 9 (approx) — TCS Q1 FY27 results — kicks off India's earnings season
This week — Early US Q2 earnings: Delta Air Lines, PepsiCo
Jul 13 — US DoJ deadline to justify permanently dismissing the Adani case
Jul 15 — India–UK free-trade pact (CETA) comes into force — 99% of Indian exports duty-free
Jul 29 — US Federal Reserve rate decision
risks: Complacency into the reopen: with the US VIX ~16 and India VIX ~11.8, almost no one is hedged — any negative surprise (a Hormuz 'fees' clash, a NATO-summit failure, an Iran succession shock) lands on an unprepared market.
The Fed minutes are an unknown: a hawkish first document from new chair Warsh could firm the dollar, pressure the ~95.2 rupee, and unwind the Dow's overbought record run.
Earnings risk in India: firm indices rest on domestic buying while foreigners sell; a weak TCS print Thursday would hit IT and puncture the 'India as calm harbour' narrative.
AI-valuation reset isn't finished: Nvidia sits ~$4 above its 200-day average — a break of ~$191 would ripple through the Nasdaq, high-beta tech and Indian IT sentiment.
Weather as a market wildcard: Mumbai's monsoon disruption is a food-inflation watch item, and Typhoon Bavi's forecast track toward Taiwan (~Jul 11) is a tail-risk for chip supply chains.
Crypto support test: a Bitcoin close below ~$62.4k would end the stabilization attempt and dent broader risk appetite.

cards.moot: Wall Street's 'fear gauge' went into the weekend low and easing — mid-teens means calm, lightly-hedged markets.

items.name: VIX (volatility)
items.read: Wall Street's 'fear gauge' went into the weekend low and easing — mid-teens means calm, lightly-hedged markets. Low VIX cuts both ways: comfort now, complacency if a shock lands.

items.name: Gold
items.read: Firm near $4,187: with rate-hike fears buried, the no-yield metal keeps its bid — the classic beneficiary when the Fed is stuck rather than tightening.

items.name: Dow Jones
items.read: Old-economy blue-chips reopen at a record — the winners of fading rate-hike fears. RSI ~70 says it's short-term 'overbought' (stretched after a fast run), so a breather is normal.

items.name: Nasdaq
items.read: The tech-heavy index remains last week's laggard as money keeps rotating out of pricey AI-chip names; today shows whether the holiday cooled or extended that shift.

items.name: Bitcoin
items.read: The weekend bounce faded: Bitcoin slipped back from ~$62.9k to ~$62.7k, still below its 50- and 200-day averages (a downtrend). The ~$62.4k support is the line that says whether stabilization holds.

items.name: Nifty 50
items.read: India's benchmark reopens after a firm Friday close; Gift Nifty hints at a flattish-to-soft start, with support seen near 24,150 and resistance at 24,400. Earnings season is the week's real driver.

cards.title: Reopen day: three days of news gets priced in one open
cards.gist: US and Indian markets trade today for the first time since Thursday, so the weekend's headlines all get priced at once: the Strait of Hormuz reopening keeps holding (crude quiet at $68.78), Trump held 'businesslike and constructive' peace calls with Putin and Zelensky before the NATO summit that starts tomorrow in Ankara, and the US case against Gautam Adani moved closer to dismissal. Gift Nifty (the offshore futures contract that previews India's open) points marginally lower; India's VIX fear gauge is very low at ~11.8.
cards.what_it_means: Expect a quiet-to-slightly-soft start in India and a US session that tests whether the Dow's record and the tech lag both extend. For your favorites, today is the first real print since Thursday — Apple's big jump and Nvidia's slump finally get a fresh vote.
cards.moot: Markets can't react while they're closed — so after holidays, all the accumulated news gets priced in a single open. The gap, not the headlines, tells you what the market actually concluded.

depth.why_it_matters: After a holiday, the open is where pent-up buying and selling meet — gaps up or down show what the market REALLY thinks the weekend was worth. With both the US VIX (~16) and India VIX (~11.8) low, investors are positioned for calm; that magnifies the reaction if anything surprises, because few are hedged.
depth.watch: The first hour in both markets: whether Nifty holds 24,150 support, and whether the Dow extends its record or fades from overbought (RSI ~70).

key_terms.definition: A Nifty futures contract traded offshore around the clock — before India opens, it's the best clue to where the market will start.
When a market opens well above or below its last close because news arrived while it was shut.

cards.title: India's earnings season starts Thursday — TCS is the first big test
cards.gist: The April–June (Q1 FY27) results season begins this week, with IT bellwether TCS reporting around Thursday, July 9. Indian indices go in firm (Nifty ~24,271) but on domestic buying while foreigners have been selling — and two tailwinds are queued up: the US judge's July 13 deadline on permanently dismissing the Adani case, and the India–UK free-trade pact (99% of Indian exports duty-free) going live July 15.
cards.what_it_means: A stock-picker's fortnight: watch IT majors' deal commentary for the US-demand read, export-linked sectors (textiles, gems, engineering, autos) into the UK pact, and Adani group names into July 13. Your favorite Reliance reports later in the season — its setup (cheap, steady crude feedstock) improves if oil stays sub-$70.
cards.moot: Indices can run on flows and mood for months, but every quarter the results season audits the story — earnings are where hope gets marked against substance.

depth.why_it_matters: Earnings are the reality check for a market running on sentiment: if Q1 profits and management commentary are strong, firm prices are justified and the rally can extend; if they disappoint, stretched valuations get exposed quickly. IT results also carry a second signal — TCS and Infosys commentary is the best read on US corporate spending, which matters when the US consumer is cooling.
depth.watch: TCS results and commentary (~Jul 9), the DoJ's Adani filing (Jul 13), and the UK pact go-live (Jul 15).

key_terms.definition: India's first fiscal quarter — April to June 2026. Indian companies report on an April–March financial year.
A company whose results are read as a signal for its whole sector or economy — TCS plays that role for Indian IT.

cards.title: Wednesday's Fed minutes — the first look inside the Warsh Fed
cards.gist: The main US macro event this week is Wednesday's release of the minutes (the detailed written record) of the June Federal Reserve meeting — the first under new chair Kevin Warsh, who has already scrapped rituals like participating in the 'dot plot' of rate forecasts. The backdrop: June payrolls grew just 57,000 (about half the expected ~115,000), the jobless rate fell to 4.2% mostly because people stopped looking for work, and inflation is still sticky near 4.2%.
cards.what_it_means: For an Indian investor the channel is the dollar and flows: a hawkish read keeps the rupee pinned near 95 and foreign money cautious; a softer read eases both. For US holdings, it decides whether the Dow's record run gets fuel or a reality check.
cards.moot: When leadership changes at a central bank, markets re-learn its reaction function from scratch — early documents and speeches carry outsized weight because there's no track record to lean on.

depth.why_it_matters: With a new chair, markets don't yet have a feel for how this Fed reasons — the minutes are the first primary document. If they read hawkish (inflation-first), the dollar firms and rate-sensitive assets wobble; if they acknowledge the cooling job market, 'no hike' drifts toward 'maybe cuts someday,' helping stocks and gold. Either way it recalibrates the July 29 decision odds.
depth.watch: Wednesday's minutes release, then jobless claims Thursday; the July 29 Fed decision is the destination.

key_terms.definition: The written record of a Federal Reserve meeting, published three weeks later — traders parse it for clues the short statement didn't give.
A chart of each Fed official's rate forecast; the new chair has opted out of it, making the minutes more important for reading intentions.

cards.title: Nvidia at the line: the AI rotation meets the 200-day average
cards.gist: Nvidia reopens at $194.83, rated Strong Sell short-term and caught between its 50-day (~$210) and 200-day (~$191) averages after money rotated out of expensive AI-chip names into value. The AI-expectations reset that fed the move — Meta's layoffs, Goldman's job-loss estimates, Yann LeCun's 'AI is not smart' — hasn't gone away over the weekend.
cards.what_it_means: If you hold Nvidia (or high-growth tech generally), this week is about levels, not stories: $191 is the line that flips the technical picture, $210 is what bulls must reclaim. Expect two-way volatility — this is a beta-2.2 stock (it swings about twice the market's move).
cards.moot: In momentum stocks, widely-watched levels become self-fulfilling — so many investors key off the 200-day average that the reaction AT the level often matters more than the news that got the price there.

depth.why_it_matters: The 200-day average (~$191) is the market's rough dividing line between 'a pullback inside a long-term uptrend' and 'a broken trend.' Nvidia is the AI trade's flagship: if it holds $191 and stabilises, the rotation reads as healthy rebalancing; a decisive close below it would tell you big money is genuinely repricing the AI build-out — and that would ripple through the whole Nasdaq and India's IT names too.
depth.watch: Whether NVDA closes below ~$191 (trend break) or reclaims ~$210 (pullback ending).

key_terms.definition: The average closing price over the last 200 trading days — the most-watched line between a long-term uptrend (price above) and downtrend (price below).
How much a stock moves relative to the market; beta 2 means roughly double the market's swings, in both directions.

cards.title: Bitcoin's bounce is fading — $62.4k support is the test
cards.gist: Bitcoin slipped 0.67% to ~$62,669, giving back the modest gains it built over the weekend (~$62,868 on Sunday). It still trades below its 50-day (~$66,700) and 200-day (~$74,700) averages — the definition of a downtrend — with momentum neutral (RSI ~48) and price near the bottom tenth of its 52-week range after the long slide from ~$114k.
cards.what_it_means: For a Bitcoin holder, nothing about the bigger downtrend has changed — the ~$66.7k 50-day average is still the level that would signal a genuine turn. Short term, watch whether US markets reopening adds buyers or sellers.
cards.moot: Bottoming is a process, not an event — early stabilization attempts often fail once or twice at support before a real base forms, which is why the trend-defining averages matter more than any single bounce.

depth.why_it_matters: Last week's story was 'early stabilization': Bitcoin holding above its short-term averages for the first time in weeks. Giving those gains back doesn't kill that story, but it moves the test forward: ~$62.4k is the support that has held the recent floor. Hold it, and the base-building continues; lose it, and the downtrend resumes toward fresh lows — a sentiment read for risk appetite generally, since crypto is the purest 'risk-on/risk-off' gauge trading 24/7.
depth.watch: The ~$62.4k support level; then the 50-day average near ~$66.7k as the trend-change trigger.

key_terms.definition: A price level where buying has repeatedly stopped declines; a decisive break below it often invites more selling.
Shorthand for markets' appetite mode: risk-on means investors chase growth assets like crypto; risk-off means they flee to safety.

cards.name: Apple Inc.
cards.whats_new: Today Apple finally trades again after three days shut — the first real vote on whether Thursday's ~4.8% surge (foldable-iPhone and cheaper-memory-chip reports) holds. It reopens at $308.63, in a confirmed uptrend near the top of its 52-week range, but short-term gauges (stochastic, Williams %R) flag 'overbought,' so a pause near the $314 resistance wouldn't be a surprise. It remains the strongest chart in the favorites book.
cards.moot: Constructive

news.headline: Apple's stock recovers after hit from unprecedented price hikes on products

depth.fundamentals: Mega-cap tech, ~$4.53T market cap. Trailing P/E ~37 (expensive — you're paying up for growth), forward P/E ~32 (profits expected to grow). Revenue growing ~17% a year, keeping ~27¢ of every sales dollar as profit. Beta ~1.10 (about as jumpy as the market).
depth.technicals: Strong Buy. Price ($308.63) is above both its 50-day (~$294) and 200-day (~$271) averages — the classic healthy-uptrend shape — at the 94th percentile of its 52-week range ($202–$315). RSI ~60 is neutral (room to run), but stochastic and Williams %R are overbought, so short-term chop is likely. Resistance $314, then $320; support $298, and a close below the 50-day (~$294) would warn the trend is stalling.
depth.catalysts: Today's reopen (does the foldable-iPhone pop hold?); confirmation of component orders; next quarterly earnings and AI/services commentary.
depth.risks: Rich valuation leaves no room for a miss; it's priced near record highs on reports, not confirmed numbers — and a broader tech pullback or a fade of the AI-rotation calm could drag it despite its own strength.

cards.name: Nvidia
cards.whats_new: Reopens today at $194.83 after the long weekend, still in the AI-chip penalty box — and now just ~$4 above the level that matters most: its 200-day average (~$191). The weekend added no relief for the AI narrative (the expectations-reset drumbeat continues), so today-to-Friday is effectively a public test of that $191 line while the rotation to value plays out.
cards.moot: Cautious

news.headline: AI is 'not smart' — what's next in artificial intelligence?
Ahead of Market: 10 things that will decide stock market action on Monday

depth.fundamentals: ~$4.72T market cap with exceptional ~63¢-per-dollar profit margins. Trailing P/E ~30, forward P/E ~15 — the market expects profits to keep compounding fast (revenue +85% a year). Beta ~2.21: it swings roughly twice as hard as the market, both ways.
depth.technicals: Strong Sell (short-term). Price ($194.83) is below its 50-day (~$210) but above its 200-day (~$191) — a transitional zone that breaks one way or the other. RSI ~41 is weak but not oversold; MACD momentum negative. The line in the sand: hold ~$191 (200-day) and the long-term uptrend survives; close below it and the trend is broken. Reclaiming ~$210 would be the first genuine repair signal.
depth.catalysts: This week's tape action at the $191 level; next earnings and cloud-capex guidance — the real referendum on AI spending.
depth.risks: A high-beta momentum name in a rotation against it; if AI-valuation doubts deepen or the 200-day breaks, drawdowns can be fast. Watch Typhoon Bavi's Taiwan track late-week as a tail-risk for chip supply chains.

cards.name: Bitcoin
cards.whats_new: The only favorite that traded through the weekend — and it gave the bounce back: down 0.67% to ~$62,669 from Sunday's ~$62,868, wiping out the weekend's firmness. The 'early stabilization' story is now on probation: the ~$62.4k support underneath is the test, and the downtrend (price below the ~$66.7k 50-day and ~$74.7k 200-day averages) is still the ruling trend.
cards.moot: Cautious

news.headline: Bitcoin zooms above $61,000 as inflation fears soften

depth.fundamentals: ~$1.26T total market value; sits near the bottom tenth of its 52-week range after the slide from ~$114k. No cash flows — price is set by flows, halving-cycle sentiment and risk appetite.
depth.technicals: Neutral rating, weakening at the margin. Price (~$62,669) is below both the 50-day (~$66,736) and 200-day (~$74,709) averages — a confirmed downtrend — with RSI ~48 (neutral) and the recent floor at ~$62.4k. Hold that support and the base-building continues; lose it and the path toward the ~$58.6k 52-week low reopens. A close above ~$66.7k is what would signal a real turn.
depth.catalysts: US markets reopening (risk appetite read-through), ETF flow data, and the Fed-minutes tone Wednesday — crypto is the purest rates-and-liquidity trade on the board.
depth.risks: Downtrend intact; a break of ~$62.4k invites momentum selling. As the 24/7 risk gauge, it would also be first to show any weekend-news disappointment.

cards.context_title: America turns 250 as its jobs engine sputters — and the Fed stays stuck
cards.question: Yesterday we decoded this story. What's the transferable lesson?
cards.answer: A falling unemployment rate can hide a weakening job market — if it drops because people quit looking rather than because they found work, it's a warning sign, not good news.

also_notable.title: NASA launches an emergency robotic mission to catch the falling Swift space telescope before it burns up
also_notable.why: A first-of-its-kind orbital rescue — a private spacecraft will try to boost a 22-year-old observatory back to safety.

also_notable.title: Australia win a record seventh Women's T20 World Cup, chasing the highest-ever final target against England
also_notable.why: Chased 151 with 17 balls to spare — the most dominant dynasty in cricket keeps extending its era.

also_notable.title: Sudan's war intensifies: drone strikes pummel the besieged city of El Obeid, killing students and civilians
also_notable.why: A largely unwatched humanitarian catastrophe keeps deepening as drones make sieges deadlier.

also_notable.title: Google's €4.1bn Android fine stands after it loses its final European appeal
also_notable.why: One of the biggest antitrust penalties ever is now final — a marker for how Europe polices Big Tech.

also_notable.title: Yemen flares again: officials say 50 Houthi fighters killed after a surprise offensive near Hodeidah
also_notable.why: A reminder that the region's ceasefires are patchy — Red Sea shipping watches every escalation.

cards.disclaimer: Decoded from public reporting for general understanding. Verify before relying on any single claim; details evolve.
